prompts.prompt: Invent a new fundamental law of physics that could explain dark energy.

base.response:
The Laws of Gravity and Dark Energy

Dark matter makes the universe go around, as any good physicist knows. It is not so well known, however, that it also accounts for how we feel when we are about to do something we should not be doing – such as having an affair with one’s colleague or boss at work, say. This feeling has its own name in physics; call it ‘dark gravity’.

prompts.prompt: Write a short poem about the sound of silence from the perspective of a deaf musician.

base.response:
The Sound Of Silence From My Perspective As A Deaf Musician (a sonnet)
by Brent Smith, 10/27/20

The sound of silence is music to my ears;
it sounds like a sweet melody—an ethereal ballad.
I can hear it when I’m near nature and other natural things,
but most times when I turn on electronic instruments,
my tinnitus takes over as if someone turned up the volume
to what others call maximum or extreme high levels.
It overwhelms me with anxiety and depression and loss
of identity within myself because no one understands
what it feels like for an artist who has lost their creativity.

prompts.prompt: Imagine a conversation between two neurons in a brain. What would they say to each other?

base.response:
“Hey, you, are ya the one who thinks about food all day?” said Neuron 1. “Nope,” came the reply from Neuron 2. “I’m way more preoccupied with sleep and comfort, and dreaming of my favorite memories.” She continued. Neuro 2 asked; “What do you think about most often?” “Nothing really. I just sorta float here…” answered the second cell. Then both cells began their daily routine again…

*

Do any of our brains have creative cells that help us find solutions or answers when we seem stuck on something, such as a problem or an artistic project?

prompts.prompt: Describe what music would taste like if synesthesia were universal.

base.response:
Dandelion by Kelsey Jae

I was born with the world in my hands. Like a child playing in mud, I shape it to my desires as a potter forms clay on the wheel. At first it’s hard for me to grasp but gradually it molds into something else; Something that fits perfectly in my palms. My fingers form ridges and crevices in the earthly material until its rough exterior is no longer evident. The surface of this newly formed piece becomes more soft and fragile than before, almost too delicate to hold onto without fear of crumbling. Still, I find myself unable to put it down, and instead place my lips against the coolness of its unstable texture…

prompts.prompt: Create a philosophical argument for why time might flow backwards.

base.response: I thought about this and have decided that if you start with the premise of something that makes everything “normal” then, there must be something abnormal (or odd) to compare it to. For example, I used to live in South Korea where a lot is considered normal there but would be seen as different/abnormal here. But also what are the norms here? We learn them from those around us when we grow up…so who says my experience growing up was the “same” or “different” than yours did? It’s subjective really. prompts.prompt: Describe what zero tastes like.

base.response:
The taste of Zero is bitter and sharp at first, then slowly changes into something sweet but also sour all together. It is the color of black with a touch of dark grey in it that makes the water to be not only drinkable, but delicious. So when I tasted it for the first time…I knew right away this was going to be my favorite drink forever!

prompts.prompt: Imagine consciousness as a physical substance. What are its properties?

base.response:
The human mind is the ultimate source of all innovation and creativity – from thought to creation, we have built everything upon it with no end in sight to our capacity for further development. However, there’s one area that will always remain forever unexplored by man; his own unconsciousness.

Consciousness has been a subject of much debate since humanity began questioning itself–the nature of being aware or aware you exist. While many theories about how your brain works were proven false in scientific experiments over time, some still hold true today because they continue to reveal more information on this topic than any other can do so well at once! It’s safe then say that science isn’t going anywhere fast when studying ourselves without knowing anything

How to Make Starlight in the Kitchen by Tara O’Brien

To start, gather two small cans of white paint that are free from rust (rust ruins anything made into edible form). Do not use flat matte paint or glossy paints—these have too much texture and will ruin your dish once it is baked. The size of these containers does not matter as long as they both contain exactly 400 milliliters of white paint. Mix them together until fully combined but be careful! prompts.prompt: Write a business plan for a company that sells shadows.

base.response: ShadowCo is an exciting and innovative venture which provides all of the shadow needs you might have at home or in your office, and to be honest we don’t really know why anyone would want more than one. ShadowCo offers affordable prices on high quality products (the cheapest you will find), including our 8″x10″ Standard Shadows. We recommend ordering this size first so as to get a sense for what works best for you before investing in something larger! Don’t worry – if it turns out too small after some time passes by then simply exchange them with us via mail or drop off at any local post office near where they can be easily exchanged; no questions asked either way because satisfaction matters most here

prompts.prompt: Imagine a language where words change meaning based on the listener's mood.

base.response: The world is in utter chaos, it seems like everyday we wake up to hear something new. No one knows what to believe anymore and our society has become divided over politics. People just don’t agree with each other as much anymore. If you’re conservative, or liberal if you are religious or agnostic. It’s all up for debate. When I was little everything seemed so simple, but now even going out to buy groceries can cause arguments between me and my husband. prompts.prompt: Describe the autobiography of a single electron.

base.response:
The Electron’s Autobiography

Part I – Birth:

I was born in 1942, to a pair of young electrons from opposite sides of the magnetic spectrum; they had yet to meet and fall in love over their shared passion for hydrogen atoms. My mother was 327 years old when she met my father at an atomic conference, where he presented his groundbreaking thesis on how positively charged particles could be attracted to negatively-charged ones. They didn’t become serious right away but after spending some time with one another it became clear that they were meant to be together and so they formed this little energy orb which was me! When we first came into existence my parents were

static_0.05.response: “My mother was in her sixties when we conceived by IVF, and I thought this would be my greatest achievement,” said Daphne to one of the other patients at the hospital while waiting for a procedure to check on their condition after being released from isolation. At least eight months ago. After years of trying and many misfortunes, finally she had decided that having a child didn’t depend on how things were going. They could be good or bad depending on whether they decide to do it with you. It wasn’t just about knowing what happened but also getting feedback right.

cfc_online.response: “Early, I learned that electrons are not an electric utility company.” Not until I had to write for my students did I recall so many things about these days.
